verses.text: Jacob travels east. He comes to a well in a field, with three flocks lying near it. A great stone covers the well's mouth — the shepherds wait until all the flocks gather before rolling it away. He pauses at the same kind of well where his mother was found.

Jacob travels east. He comes to a well in a field, with three flocks lying near it. A great stone covers the well's mouth — the shepherds wait until all the flocks gather before rolling it away. He pauses at the same kind of well where his mother was found.

Jacob travels east. He comes to a well in a field, with three flocks lying near it. A great stone covers the well's mouth — the shepherds wait until all the flocks gather before rolling it away. He pauses at the same kind of well where his mother was found.

He asks the shepherds: where are you from? Haran. Do you know Laban? Yes. Is he well? Yes — and look, his daughter Rachel is coming now with the sheep. The answers arrive in the same breath as the woman they describe.

He asks the shepherds: where are you from? Haran. Do you know Laban? Yes. Is he well? Yes — and look, his daughter Rachel is coming now with the sheep. The answers arrive in the same breath as the woman they describe.

He asks the shepherds: where are you from? Haran. Do you know Laban? Yes. Is he well? Yes — and look, his daughter Rachel is coming now with the sheep. The answers arrive in the same breath as the woman they describe.
Why do you sit here so early in the day? Jacob asks. Water the sheep and let them feed. They answer: we cannot, until all the flocks gather and the stone is rolled away. Local custom holds them back.
Why do you sit here so early in the day? Jacob asks. Water the sheep and let them feed. They answer: we cannot, until all the flocks gather and the stone is rolled away. Local custom holds them back.

While he is still speaking, Rachel arrives with her father's sheep — she is a shepherdess herself. Jacob walks over, rolls the great stone away by himself, and waters the flock for his uncle. Love makes light work of heavy stones.

While he is still speaking, Rachel arrives with her father's sheep — she is a shepherdess herself. Jacob walks over, rolls the great stone away by himself, and waters the flock for his uncle. Love makes light work of heavy stones.
Then Jacob kisses Rachel, lifts up his voice, and weeps. After all the deceiving and fleeing and dreaming, his first response in his mother's homeland is tears.

He tells her he is her father's relative, Rebekah's son. She runs to tell her father. Laban comes running out, embraces him, kisses him, brings him home. After a month of welcome, the working arrangement begins.

He tells her he is her father's relative, Rebekah's son. She runs to tell her father. Laban comes running out, embraces him, kisses him, brings him home. After a month of welcome, the working arrangement begins.

He tells her he is her father's relative, Rebekah's son. She runs to tell her father. Laban comes running out, embraces him, kisses him, brings him home. After a month of welcome, the working arrangement begins.

Laban asks: just because you are family, should you serve me for nothing? Name your wages. Now Genesis introduces his two daughters: Leah, with tender eyes — gentle eyes, perhaps weak — and Rachel, beautiful in form and face. One verse sets up the whole conflict ahead.

Laban asks: just because you are family, should you serve me for nothing? Name your wages. Now Genesis introduces his two daughters: Leah, with tender eyes — gentle eyes, perhaps weak — and Rachel, beautiful in form and face. One verse sets up the whole conflict ahead.

Laban asks: just because you are family, should you serve me for nothing? Name your wages. Now Genesis introduces his two daughters: Leah, with tender eyes — gentle eyes, perhaps weak — and Rachel, beautiful in form and face. One verse sets up the whole conflict ahead.
I will serve thee seven years for Rachel, your younger daughter. Jacob names a steep price. Love makes him generous; he doesn't bargain.
It is better that I give her to thee than to another, says Laban. Jacob works seven years, and it seemed to him only a few days, because of the love he had for her. Time bends inside affection.
It is better that I give her to thee than to another, says Laban. Jacob works seven years, and it seemed to him only a few days, because of the love he had for her. Time bends inside affection.
Give me my wife — my days are fulfilled. Laban gathers the men of the place and makes a wedding feast. The bride is veiled, as Rebekah once was. Nothing yet seems amiss.
Give me my wife — my days are fulfilled. Laban gathers the men of the place and makes a wedding feast. The bride is veiled, as Rebekah once was. Nothing yet seems amiss.
In the evening, Laban brings Leah to Jacob's tent. Veiled, in the dim light of a tent, the substitution succeeds. He goes in unto her. Laban gives Zilpah as Leah's handmaid.
In the evening, Laban brings Leah to Jacob's tent. Veiled, in the dim light of a tent, the substitution succeeds. He goes in unto her. Laban gives Zilpah as Leah's handmaid.
In the morning, behold — it was Leah. The man who once deceived his own father by feel and disguise is now deceived by feel and disguise. The deceiver has met his Laban. Why have you tricked me?
In our country, says Laban, the younger is not given before the firstborn. Fulfill her bridal week, and we will give you the other too — for another seven years of service. Jacob is doubly bound now.
In our country, says Laban, the younger is not given before the firstborn. Fulfill her bridal week, and we will give you the other too — for another seven years of service. Jacob is doubly bound now.

Jacob keeps Leah's week, then receives Rachel also as a wife. Bilhah is given as Rachel's handmaid. Jacob loves Rachel more than Leah — and serves another seven years. One man, two sisters, fourteen years. The household is structured for grief from the start.

Jacob keeps Leah's week, then receives Rachel also as a wife. Bilhah is given as Rachel's handmaid. Jacob loves Rachel more than Leah — and serves another seven years. One man, two sisters, fourteen years. The household is structured for grief from the start.

Jacob keeps Leah's week, then receives Rachel also as a wife. Bilhah is given as Rachel's handmaid. Jacob loves Rachel more than Leah — and serves another seven years. One man, two sisters, fourteen years. The household is structured for grief from the start.

When the LORD saw that Leah was hated — loved less than her sister — He opened her womb. But Rachel was barren. God's eye goes immediately to the wife least wanted. Heaven's first answer to family pain is given to the one with the least.
Leah bears a son and names him Reuben — see, a son. For the LORD hath looked on my affliction, she says, now my husband will love me. The name is a hope offered to a man who hasn't asked for it.
She bears a second son, Simeon — meaning heard. Because the LORD hath heard that I was hated. Each child's name records the prayer she is still praying.
Third son: Levi — joined. Now this time, my husband will be joined unto me, because I have born him three sons. She names her aching toward intimacy. Levi's tribe will later be set apart for God.

Fourth son: Judah — praise. Now will I praise the LORD. The name is no longer about her husband. Something has shifted — Leah turns from her marriage to God. From this son will come David, and eventually Jesus.
